name: Alpha Corp
title: Alpha Corp UBO Structure
briefing: Alpha Corp has applied for onboarding. Its primary shareholder holds 26 percent through a shell company registered in the British Virgin Islands. Build out the UBO tree to identify all three entities, then screen each one before you decide.
rationale: The 26 percent stake is routed through a British Virgin Islands shell company, and the ultimate beneficial owner returns a possible sanctions match with a mismatched date of birth. Neither fact alone is sufficient justification, together they require rejection or a request for more information.

related_guide.title: The Onboarding Dilemma
related_guide.reason: More on judgement calls like this one

name: Retail Customer
title: Document Authenticity Flag
briefing: A retail customer has submitted an ID document where the MRZ text font does not match standard passport typography. Identify the customer and screen them, then decide.
rationale: Screening returned no match, but a clean screening result does not close an investigation on its own. The document's MRZ typography does not match standard ICAO passport font, an independent authenticity red flag that screening cannot detect.

related_guide.title: The Onboarding Dilemma
related_guide.reason: More on document and identity red flags

name: Tech Startup Beta LLC
title: Tech Startup Beta LLC
briefing: Tech Startup Beta LLC has applied for onboarding, with all UBOs based locally and business operations that match its public profile. Identify every entity and screen each one, then decide.
rationale: Both founders are local and fully verified, and business operations match the public profile. Founder B carries a disclosed PEP connection, correctly identified and weighed rather than overlooked, but screening returns clean and the connection presents low risk. Screening every identified entity returns clean, this remains the case where identification and screening clear without qualification, the PEP status is a factor considered, not a blocker.

related_guide.title: Methodology
related_guide.reason: What a clean screening result actually means

name: Apex Consulting
title: Apex Consulting — Storefront Legitimacy Check
briefing: An individual registers as a sole trader offering web design consultation services. The registered business address matches a known corporate virtual mailbox provider in London currently hosting over 4,000 active entities. The associated website domain was registered six days ago and consists almost entirely of template placeholder copy with no verifiable client work shown. The applicant's personal identity documents pass all authenticity checks with no discrepancies.
rationale: The applicant's own identity is genuine, this is not a document fraud or impersonation case. The risk sits in the business profile itself: a shared virtual mailbox address hosting thousands of unrelated entities, a domain registered days before onboarding, and no evidence of a functioning business behind the registration. This pattern matches known transaction laundering storefronts, shells used to route card-not-present processing volume that has nothing to do with the stated business. The correct action is to request historical processing history, signed client agreements, or other evidence the business genuinely operates before approving payment processing access.

related_guide.title: Transaction Monitoring
related_guide.reason: More on spotting transaction laundering patterns

name: Horizon Shipping Logistics Ltd
title: Horizon Shipping — Relational Sanctions Exposure
briefing: A mid-sized shipping logistics operator applies for onboarding. The entity itself screens completely clean against all global sanctions and watchlists. Corporate ownership mapping shows one shareholder, holding a 30% stake, is the adult son of a senior government minister in Ruritania who was designated under an asset-freeze regime eighteen months ago. Direct sanctions screening of the son himself returns no match, he is not named on any designated persons list in his own right.
rationale: This case tests whether a clean direct screening result is treated as the end of the analysis or the start of it. The son is not designated, so an automatic reject cannot be justified on sanctions grounds alone, ownership by a family member of a designated person is not itself a listing trigger. But a 30% stake held by the close relative of a currently sanctioned official is a well established nominee and asset-shielding pattern. The correct action is to request further information to establish whether the son is acting as an independent beneficial owner or as a proxy holding the stake on his father's behalf, before any onboarding decision is made. Approving on the strength of a clean screening hit alone ignores the relational risk; rejecting outright on relationship alone is not supported by the facts as they stand.

title: Authorised Push Payment Scam
briefing: A customer's account shows a large outbound transfer request. Step through the account activity below, then decide.

timeline.title: Login
timeline.body: Customer logs in from their usual device. IP and location match prior sessions. Nothing unusual yet.

timeline.title: Transfer request
timeline.body: £45,000 outbound transfer requested to a domestic business account. The account was opened 41 hours ago.

timeline.title: Device check
timeline.body: Session originates from the customer's known iPad. Device fingerprint matches history, no spoofing detected.

timeline.title: Support chat log
timeline.body: Customer contacts support mid transfer, sounds panicked, references an urgent "crypto bond opportunity" someone told them about.

rationale: Every individual signal here looks clean in isolation, that's exactly what makes this pattern dangerous. Biometrics and device fingerprint are genuine. The risk sits entirely in the behavioural pattern: a large, urgent transfer to a brand-new account, combined with a support call describing textbook social-engineering language. This is precisely the scenario the UK's mandatory APP fraud reimbursement regime was built around. Since 7 October 2024, payment providers have been required to reimburse eligible APP scam victims up to £85,000, with the cost split 50/50 between the sending and receiving payment provider. The Payment Systems Regulator's own independent evaluation, published 1 July 2026, found the policy cut in-scope Faster Payments scam losses by roughly £73m a year. That regime exists precisely because catching this pattern before the money moves is now a direct, shared financial liability for the firm, not just a customer protection nicety. Freezing the transfer here isn't just good practice, it's the decision the regulatory framework is actively designed to reward.

related_guide.title: Screening Alert Survival Guide
related_guide.reason: More on triaging alerts like this one

title: Session Hijacking and Impossible Travel
briefing: A customer's account shows two checkout sessions within minutes of each other. Step through the account activity below, then decide.

timeline.title: Ordinary purchase
timeline.body: Customer completes a routine low-value transaction from their usual session, familiar device, familiar London-based IP. Nothing unusual.

timeline.title: Password changed
timeline.body: Account password is modified, roughly three and a half minutes after the first transaction and shortly before the next event.

timeline.title: Second checkout attempt
timeline.body: A £2,400 checkout request for micro-token digital gift vouchers is initiated four minutes after the first transaction, from an Android device on a residential proxy IP geolocating to Lagos, Nigeria.

timeline.title: Geolocation check
timeline.body: The gap between the two sessions is physically impossible to travel in the time elapsed. Device fingerprint also changed entirely between the two transactions.

rationale: Three independent signals point the same direction. A password change immediately before a high-value transaction attempt is a classic account takeover marker, not routine account hygiene. The geographic jump from London to Lagos inside minutes is not physically possible, this is textbook impossible travel, a strong, well-established indicator that the second session belongs to someone other than the account holder. Digital gift vouchers specifically are a common cash-out mechanism in account takeover fraud, they convert stolen access into a liquid, hard-to-trace asset almost immediately. The correct action is to kill the session and force step-up, out-of-band verification before anything proceeds, not just decline the single transaction, since the account itself is compromised, not just this one checkout attempt.

title: Transaction Laundering (Digital Storefront Mimicry)
briefing: A small merchant account shows an unusual overnight spike in processing volume. Step through the account activity below, then decide.

timeline.title: Normal trading pattern
timeline.body: A small merchant account, registered as a handmade candle retailer, processes a steady, low volume of transactions in the £15 to £40 range, consistent with its stated business, during normal daytime hours.

timeline.title: Volume surge
timeline.body: Processing volume begins climbing sharply overnight, eventually reaching roughly 1,200% above the account's established baseline for the weekend.

timeline.title: Anomalous transaction values
timeline.body: Transaction values shift entirely away from the merchant's normal retail pricing, round figures of £500, £1,000, and £2,500 begin appearing, nothing resembling candle retail pricing.

timeline.title: Card testing signal
timeline.body: International card authorisation failure rate for this merchant exceeds 65% during the same overnight window, consistent with stolen card numbers being tested for validity before a larger fraudulent charge.

rationale: Every element of this pattern is inconsistent with the merchant's stated business, small-value handmade goods sold during normal hours. Round, high-value transaction amounts appearing overnight are the classic tell of transaction laundering, a storefront that looks legitimate on paper being used to process payments for a completely different, often illicit business the card networks would otherwise decline to onboard directly. The card testing failure rate above 65% adds a second, distinct risk layer on top: this account is very likely also being used to validate stolen card numbers before they're used elsewhere, a pattern often called carding. Two separate fraud typologies converging on one merchant account in the same overnight window isn't a coincidence worth waiting on, it's grounds to suspend the account and investigate before further volume passes through it.

title: Structuring (Smurfing)
briefing: A dormant personal account suddenly receives a rapid sequence of cash deposits. Step through the deposit history below, then decide.

timeline.title: First deposit
timeline.body: A dormant personal current account, minimal transaction history for the past 14 months, receives a cash deposit of £2,400 at a branch ATM.

timeline.title: Second deposit
timeline.body: A second cash deposit of £2,350 is made at a different ATM location, in the same municipal area.

timeline.title: Two further deposits
timeline.body: Two further cash deposits, £2,480 and £2,290, are made within the same morning at two more separate ATM locations.

timeline.title: Fifth deposit
timeline.body: A fifth deposit of £2,410 completes the pattern: five deposits totalling £11,930 across five distinct locations inside roughly 36 hours, none individually large enough to be conspicuous on its own.

disposition_options.label: Reject and escalate for SAR filing

rationale: There is no automatic reporting threshold in UK law the way there is in the US, UK AML runs on suspicion-based reporting under POCA 2002, not a fixed currency transaction trigger. What matters here is the pattern itself: a dormant account suddenly receiving a rapid sequence of cash deposits, each kept below a round, conspicuous figure, spread deliberately across multiple locations in a short window. That's a classic structuring pattern regardless of any specific threshold, the deliberate fragmentation and geographic spread across the account's otherwise dormant history is itself the red flag POCA's suspicion-based regime is built to catch. The correct action is to escalate for a Suspicious Activity Report, the analyst doesn't need the deposits to cross any specific number to form a reasonable suspicion, they need the pattern, and this one is textbook.

related_guide.title: SAR Complete Guide, Part 2
related_guide.reason: More on what actually goes into a SAR
